coredata.dc:title: Robust and data-efficient battery state of charge estimation via transfer learning-enhanced physics-informed neural networks
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2026 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Hong, Seonri
Kim, Hyejin
Kim, Jonghoon
Baek, Jongbok
coredata.dc:description: Accurate state of charge (SOC) estimation is essential to ensure the reliability and safety of battery management systems. Conventional data-driven methods rely on large labeled datasets and exhibit poor generalization across battery chemistries. By contrast, physics-based models offer interpretability but require complex parameter identification and incur high computational costs. To address these challenges, this study proposed a transfer learning-enhanced physics-informed neural network (TL-PINN) framework that combined the interpretability of physics-based models with the adaptability of deep learning. The framework integrated three key components: a physics-informed neural network for SOC estimation, a TL mechanism for domain adaptation, and a hybrid loss function that balances physical consistency and data efficiency. Experimental evaluations on the nickel manganese cobalt, lithium polymer, and nickel cobalt aluminum cells demonstrated that the TL-PINN outperformed conventional methods by achieving a lower root mean square error (RMSE), faster inference (approximately 2.4 ms), and enhanced robustness to sensor noise. Notably, under data-scarce conditions, the TL-PINN reduced the RMSE by more than 63% compared with the extended Kalman filter and more than 50% compared with recurrent neural network models. Furthermore, it consistently maintained a prediction error within 2%, even with noisy measurements. These results validate TL-PINN as a robust and scalable solution for SOC estimation under practical constraints and suggest its potential extension to broader battery health diagnostics, such as SOH and remaining useful life estimation.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Physics-informed neural network for SOC estimation

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY license.

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2026 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Experimental setup for the laboratory dataset.
Voltage and current profiles of the laboratory dataset: (a) cycle and (b) reference test.
Schematic of the proposed transfer learning-enhanced physics-informed neural network (TL-PINN) framework.
First-order equivalent circuit model of the battery.
Flowchart of the proposed TL-PINN training procedure.
Baseline SOC estimation performance of different models under same domain (NMC ➔ NMC).
Across-domain SOC estimation performance under the domain shift NMC ➔ NCA.
Across-domain SOC estimation performance under the domain shift NMC ➔ LiPo.
SOC estimation performance on the NCA target domain for different training dataset sizes: (a) one subset, (b) two subsets, and (c) the full dataset.
SOC estimation performance on the LiPo target domain for different training dataset sizes: (a) one subset, (b) two subsets, and (c) the full dataset.
Transfer learning performance on the target domain for different training dataset sizes.
Training loss convergences of the RNN, PINN, and TL-PINN over epochs.
Robustness to 5% current noise and 1% voltage noise: rolling RMSE (left) and error boxplots (right) for (a) NCA and (b) LiPo.
Specifications of the training and validation datasets.
Comparison of estimated and true battery parameters.
Ablation study of TL-PINN: Comparison of pretrained initialization and random initialization.
Comparative performances of the SOC estimation models under measurement noise.

head.ce:title: Robust and data-efficient battery state of charge estimation via transfer learning-enhanced physics-informed neural networks

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Energy ICT Convergence Research Department, Energy Efficiency Research Division, Korea Institute of Energy Research, Daejeon, 16105, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Energy ICT Convergence Research Department, Energy Efficiency Research Division, Korea Institute of Energy Research, Daejeon, 16105, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Energy Storage and Conversion Laboratory, Department of Electrical Engineering, Chungnam National University, Daejeon, 34134, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Energy Storage and Conversion Laboratory, Department of Electrical Engineering, Chungnam National University, Daejeon, 34134, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Accurate state of charge (SOC) estimation is essential to ensure the reliability and safety of battery management systems. Conventional data-driven methods rely on large labeled datasets and exhibit poor generalization across battery chemistries. By contrast, physics-based models offer interpretability but require complex parameter identification and incur high computational costs. To address these challenges, this study proposed a transfer learning-enhanced physics-informed neural network (TL-PINN) framework that combined the interpretability of physics-based models with the adaptability of deep learning. The framework integrated three key components: a physics-informed neural network for SOC estimation, a TL mechanism for domain adaptation, and a hybrid loss function that balances physical consistency and data efficiency. Experimental evaluations on the nickel manganese cobalt, lithium polymer, and nickel cobalt aluminum cells demonstrated that the TL-PINN outperformed conventional methods by achieving a lower root mean square error (RMSE), faster inference (approximately 2.4 ms), and enhanced robustness to sensor noise. Notably, under data-scarce conditions, the TL-PINN reduced the RMSE by more than 63% compared with the extended Kalman filter and more than 50% compared with recurrent neural network models. Furthermore, it consistently maintained a prediction error within 2%, even with noisy measurements. These results validate TL-PINN as a robust and scalable solution for SOC estimation under practical constraints and suggest its potential extension to broader battery health diagnostics, such as SOH and remaining useful life estimation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Battery state of charge (SOC) estimation is a fundamental function of battery management systems (BMSs) and plays a crucial role in ensuring safe, efficient, and reliable battery operation in electric vehicles (EVs), energy storage systems, portable electronics, and grid-scale applications [1,2]. Accurate SOC estimation enables optimal energy utilization, extends battery lifespan, and prevents critical failures such as overcharging or deep discharging. However, achieving high-precision SOC estimation remains challenging because of the complex nonlinear dynamics of batteries, their sensitivity to environmental variations, and aging effects [3,4]. These challenges necessitate the development of robust, data-efficient, and generalizable SOC estimation methodologies capable of adapting to different battery chemistries and operating conditions.
Methods for estimating the battery SOC can be classified into three types: model-based, data-driven, and hybrid.

Model-based methods, such as equivalent circuit models (ECMs) [5] and electrochemical models [6] offer physically interpretable SOC estimation by representing internal battery dynamics through mathematical models. ECMs, including Thevenin and resistor–capacitor (RC) circuit models, provide SOC estimations with relatively low computational complexities, making them widely adopted in practical BMS implementations [7]. More sophisticated electrochemical models, such as single particle models and pseudo-two-dimensional models, capture the lithium-ion transport and reaction kinetics, enabling high-accuracy SOC predictions [8]. However, these models require extensive parameter identification that can vary owing to battery degradation, and their high computational costs limit their real-time applicability in embedded BMS platforms [9,10].

Data-driven methods, including deep learning models such as long short-term memory networks [11], convolutional neural networks [12], and transformer architectures [13–15], have shown promise in capturing the complex relationships between voltage, current, temperature, and SOC. These models can learn high-dimensional patterns from historical battery data and improve the SOC prediction accuracy. However, their performance relies heavily on the availability of large labeled datasets that are often expensive and time-consuming to acquire. Furthermore, deep learning models tend to struggle with generalization when applied to different battery chemistries, aging states, and environmental conditions, necessitating frequent retraining [16]. Another significant drawback is the lack of interpretability, i.e., the inability to ground model predictions in physically meaningful state variables such as SOC or internal resistance. Deep learning models often operate as “black boxes” without clear physical reasoning, which makes them less suitable for safety-critical applications [17].

Hybrid methods that integrate physical constraints into data-driven models have been proposed to address the limitations of both methods. These include serial models, in which physics-based and data-driven estimators are connected in sequence; parallel models that fuse independent predictions from both domains; embedded architectures, such as physics-informed neural networks (PINNs), in which physical constraints are directly incorporated into the learning process. More recently, adaptive hybrid frameworks that allow dynamic weighting between model components depending on the operating conditions have been explored.

Among these methods, the PINN embeds physical constraints into the neural network architecture, ensuring that SOC predictions remain consistent with established battery physics and thereby improving interpretability [18–20]. For instance, Tian et al. [21] proposed a physics-informed approach for SOC estimation in which voltage measurements were decomposed into physically interpretable components, and temporal consistency was enforced via ampere-hour counting. Their method improved accuracy under dynamic driving conditions, reducing RMSE and MAE by 30.89% and 64.88%, respectively. Similarly, Wang et al. [22] developed a physics-informed deep learning framework for jointly estimating SOC and capacity in EV battery packs using only two online-selected representative cells. By integrating a PINN with a closed-loop Kalman filter, their approach improved RMSE by up to 85.13% under real-world drive cycles and demonstrated strong robustness to sensor drift. Kajiura et al. [23] further extended the PINN concept by embedding system dynamics into a single integration-based loss function, enabling the estimation of unmeasurable states and the identification of system parameters using only measurable signals.

Although these studies demonstrate the potential of PINNs, their effectiveness strongly depends on the problem setting. For forward problems, PINNs can often operate in a semi-supervised manner by enforcing consistency with governing equations. However, SOC estimation is an inverse problem where the latent state variable cannot be directly constrained by physical equations alone. In such cases, a certain amount of cycle- or segment-level labeled data is required to stabilize training and avoid non-identifiability. This reliance on labeled information significantly limits applicability when datasets are scarce or when transferring across different battery chemistries, where RMSE can increase by 30–50% compared to within-chemistry training [24–26]. To address this challenge, transfer learning (TL) has been introduced to leverage pretrained models, thereby reducing the demand for extensive labeled datasets and improving adaptability across operating conditions [27,28].

Recent studies have also begun to explore the synergy between PINNs and transfer learning. For example, Papastathopoulos-Katsaros et al. [29] introduced a TL-enhanced PINN with adaptive activation functions to improve extrapolation capability, achieving up to 50% error reduction outside the training domain. Deng et al. [30] proposed a PINN-TL approach for cross-battery SOH estimation, where fine-tuning significantly reduced RMSE and MAPE compared to conventional baselines. Wang et al. [31] systematically investigated TL strategies for PINNs, comparing full fine-tuning, lightweight fine-tuning, and low-rank adaptation, and demonstrated that the choice of TL scheme critically affects convergence speed and accuracy. In the battery domain, Yeregui et al. [32] applied a two-stage PINN-TL framework to estimate electrochemical parameters in real cells, first pretraining on laboratory datasets and then adapting to field conditions.

These works confirm the growing importance of combining PINNs with TL, but most approaches remain limited to either extrapolation tasks or parameter estimation under restricted conditions. In contrast, the proposed TL-PINN framework explicitly targets SOC estimation across diverse chemistries and operational conditions, bridging physics-based interpretability with data-efficient generalization. Unlike prior hybrid methods, TL-PINN demonstrates simultaneous robustness to noisy measurements, adaptability under scarce labeled data, and consistent accuracy in cross-chemistry transfer.

Building upon these insights, this study proposes a transfer learning-enhanced physics-informed neural network (TL-PINN) framework that integrates a PINN with TL to improve SOC estimation accuracy, cross-chemistry generalization, and robustness under diverse conditions For comparative evaluation, TL-PINN was benchmarked against representative data-driven (RNN, LSTM, GRU, CNN), model-based (EKF), and hybrid (conventional PINN) baselines to ensure fairness and comprehensiveness. The main contributions of this study are as follows:
1)
Developing a novel TL-PINN framework that integrates physics-informed constraints with transfer learning for enhanced SOC estimation.
2)
Demonstrating cross-chemistry generalization, where TL-PINN adapts to different battery types without retraining, overcoming the 30–50% RMSE degradation observed in data-driven baselines.
3)
Improving data efficiency and robustness by reducing reliance on large-scale labeled datasets and maintaining superior performance under noisy and uncertain conditions.
4)
Providing a comprehensive comparison with state-of-the-art baselines across various practical scenarios, highlighting TL-PINN's distinctive advantages in accuracy, generalization, and robustness.

The remainder of this paper is organized as follows: Section 2 describes the dataset used in this study. Section 3 details the proposed TL-PINN framework, including data preprocessing, integration of physics-informed neural networks for SOC estimation, TL for data efficiency, model training and validation, and the experimental setup. Section 4 provides the experimental results and analysis, covering the generalization across battery chemistries, low-data adaptation performance, robustness to measurement noise, and overall model performance comparison. Finally, Section 5 summarizes the findings and concludes the paper.

Three battery datasets were used to validate the proposed framework. Table 1 summarizes the test materials in the datasets used in the experiments. These datasets were selected to represent diverse battery chemistries, manufacturers, and usage profiles, thereby providing a robust basis for cross-domain validation. By alternately treating each dataset as a target domain while using the other datasets for pretraining, the proposed framework was evaluated under varying distribution shifts. This cross-validation strategy enabled the assessment of the generalization capability of the model when applied to unseen battery types. First, a laboratory dataset was obtained from laboratory experiments under controlled conditions. The battery experimental platform, as shown in Fig. 1 , included batteries testing equipment, a host computer, thermal chamber, data logger, and lithium-ion battery set, and the temperature resolution was 0.1 °C. The test material was a Samsung 21,700-50E with a nominal capacity of 4900 m Ah. These battery cells had a nominal voltage of 3.6 V, a discharge cut-off voltage of 2.5 V, and a charge cut-off voltage of 4.2 V. The laboratory dataset consisted of cycling, pulse-discharge, and dynamic stress test (DST) profiles, with the ambient temperature set to 25 °C.

In the laboratory dataset, the reference test for parameter identification and the cycle profile data for model training are shown in Fig. 2 . Second, the dataset was sourced from Mendeley Data [33]. The test material was a Panasonic 18650PF with a cylindrical lithium-ion battery and nickel cobalt aluminum oxide (NCA, LiNiCoAlO2) chemistry. These battery cells had a nominal capacity of 2900 m Ah, a nominal voltage of 3.6 V, a discharge cutoff voltage of 2.5 V, and a charge cutoff voltage of 4.2 V. This was tested under the US06, HWFET, UDDS, LA92, and NN profiles. The battery was charged after each test at a 1C rate to 4.2 V, with 50 mA cut-off and battery temperature of 12–25 °C or higher. The sampling time was 0.1 s. Third, the dataset was sourced from Mendeley Data [34]. The test material was a Turnigy Graphene battery based on lithium polymer (LiPo) chemistry. These battery cells had a nominal capacity of 4900 m Ah, a nominal voltage of 3.7 V, a discharge cut-off voltage of 2.8 V, and a charge cut-off voltage of 4.2 V. The sampling time was 0.1 s. This was tested under the HWFET, UDDS, LA92, and mixed profiles. These profiles were selected to analyze the performance of each battery type under different load conditions, ensuring a comprehensive validation of the proposed framework.

The NMC dataset was used as the source domain for pre-training, whereas the NCA and LiPo datasets served as target domains to evaluate the effectiveness of TL. Each dataset contained time-series measurements of the voltage, current, and temperature collected under standard dynamic current profiles, including FUDS, US06, and DST. These datasets were used in the experiments, as described in Section 4, to assess the proposed framework in terms of generalization across battery chemistries, data efficiency under limited label availability, and robustness against sensor noise.

In this section, we describe the TL-PINN framework used for SOC estimation. The basic assumption is that we know the battery dynamic behavior and have a large experimental dataset for only the source domain. However, the proposed framework required SOC estimation for a given target domain with an uncertain small-scale size. Learning the target dataset alone is difficult because the number of samples is considerably smaller than that of the source dataset and labels may not be provided. The proposed model used two features—input current and measured voltage—to estimate the SOC and uncertain battery parameters from these features. Therefore, this study proposed a TL-PINN framework that integrated physics-based modeling with the adaptability of deep learning.

The components of the TL-PINN framework included the following:
1)
SOC estimation based on the PINN model: These networks incorporated battery electrochemical equations into the learning process to ensure physically meaningful SOC estimation.
2)
TL mechanism: A pre-trained SOC model was fine-tuned to new battery chemistries and conditions, thereby improving the data efficiency.
3)
Hybrid loss function: A multicomponent loss function that balanced empirical learning, physical constraints, and TL adaptation.

By integrating these elements, TL-PINN enhanced model generalization, reduced reliance on extensively labeled datasets, and improved robustness against sensor noise and environmental variability. Fig. 3 outlines the end-to-end workflow across five sequential steps. Step 1 involved feature extraction, including battery parameters and OCV-SOC curves from the three datasets. Step 2 began with preprocessing, including noise reduction, normalization, and data splitting. Step 3 involved pre-training of the neural network using a hybrid loss function. Step 4 implemented TL to fine-tune the target domain. Finally, Step 5 involved robust SOC and battery parameter estimations.

To ensure data integrity and consistency, a structured preprocessing pipeline was applied to the dataset. The preprocessing steps included noise reduction, normalization, and data splitting. First, noise reduction was performed using a Savitzky–Golay filter that smoothed the sensor noise while preserving essential signal characteristics [35]. The filtering process is defined as follows:
(1)
V filterd
t
=
∑
k = − K
K
c k
V
t + k
where c
k represents the filter coefficients and k determines the smoothing window size. This method effectively reduced noise while maintaining the underlying data structure. Subsequently, feature normalization was applied using min–max scaling to standardize the voltage, current, and temperature values [36]. The voltage and current values are normalized to a range of −1 to 1. The transformation can be expressed as follows:
(2)
x norm
=
2
x −
x min
x max
−
x min
− 1
where x
max and x
min represent the fixed maximum and minimum values of the voltage and current, respectively. The voltage range was [2.3, 4.3] V, and the current range was [−10,10] A. Subsequently, charge–discharge segmentation was implemented to categorize the dataset into charging, discharging, and rest phases, allowing phase-specific learning. The segmentation rule was based on the current sign classification. This classification enabled the model to learn distinct behaviors across different battery operating conditions. Finally, data augmentation was introduced to enhance the robustness of the model against noisy conditions. Synthetic perturbations were applied to simulate realistic sensor noise by modifying the voltage and current values. This can be expressed as follows:
(3)
V noise
t
= V
t
+ ε ,
I noise
t
= I
t
+ δ
ε ∼ N ( 0 ,
0.01 V
t
2
,
δ ∼ N ( 0 ,
0.05 I
t
2
where ε and δ represent voltage and current sensor noise, respectively, each modeled as zero-mean Gaussian noise with variance proportional to the magnitude of their corresponding signals. This augmentation technique improved the generalization capability of the model under varying sensor noise levels.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physics-informed neural network for SOC estimation
ce:section.ce:para:
The PINN model integrated the governing equations directly into the learning process, thereby enhancing the prediction accuracy and interpretability. A first-order ECM was used to represent the battery dynamics, as shown in Fig. 4 . This battery model was approximated using an OCV, ohmic resistance R 0, and a parallel R–C pair, where R
p and C
p represent the polarization resistance and capacitance, respectively. The state variables used to characterize the battery behavior were the SOC and diffusion voltage V
p . Each state variable is described using the following governing Eqs. [37]:
(4)
SOC
t
= SOC

(7)

= OCV

V p
+
R 0
I
t
where SOC(t) represents the SOC at time t. I(t) and V
t (t) denote the input current and terminal voltage, respectively. Q
norm denotes the nominal battery capacity. The OCV was fitted using a seventh-order polynomial. This is performed as follows:
(8)
OCV
SOC
=

k = 0

SO

where

is a polynomial coefficient that is predefined using a hybrid pulse power characterization profile. The given system was expressed as an ordinary differential equation (ODE) with two state variables, x = [x 1, x 2] = [SOC, V
p ]. To obtain the solution, the system was formulated as a nonlinear system. Here, state variable x was not measurable.

where functions f(·) and g(·) denote the mapping between the state value and the terminal voltage parameterized by λ. Also h(·) denotes the algebraic constraints of the battery model, representing instantaneous conditions such as charge conservation and current continuity that must be satisfied at all times. Specifically h(·) includes individual constraint terms corresponding to charge conservation, voltage limits, and initial conditions, respectively:

denotes the initial SOC. To ensure physical consistency during training and validation, specific voltage constraints were applied based on the battery chemistry. For NCA dataset, the voltage limits were set to

The estimated state variable,
x ̂
, is represented by a neural network F
∙
, parameterized by weight θ using input current u(t) and measured voltage y(t) as follows:
(15)

t

t − l : t
θ
where Δ and l denote the sample time and the length of the input window, respectively. The estimated state
x ̂
was integrated through function f
∙
and subsequently transformed into predicted voltage
y ̂
via function g(·) while being enforced to match observed output y over a certain duration of time. Therefore, the optimization problem with respect to θ could be solved by minimizing loss function L that quantified the error. The minimization process involved optimizing both θ and λ using the Adam [38] optimization algorithm.

The TL-PINN framework optimized SOC estimation using a multicomponent loss function.
(16)
L
to t al

0

2 2
where L
g represents the error between measured voltage y and voltage
y ̂
computed via function g(·) and L
h represents the error owing to constraints such as initial conditions and boundary conditions including the initial SOC
z 0
, voltage limits, and current continuity.

The weighting coefficients
ω g
and
ω h
are normalized factors (satisfying
ω g
+
ω h
= 1 ) introduced to balance the numerical scales of the data and physics-based losses. This prevents one term from disproportionately influencing the optimization. These coefficients control the relative importance between
L g
and
L h
. To determine an appropriate balance, a grid search was conducted over
ω g
∈ [0.1:0.1:0.9], with
ω h
= 1 −
ω g
, using RMSE on a validation set as the primary criterion. The optimal configuration (
ω g
=0.6,
ω h
=0.4) minimized RMSE while preserving physical consistency. Furthermore, a sensitivity analysis was performed by varying
ω g
and
ω h
within ±20% of the optimal values. The results showed that TL-PINN maintained stable accuracy across this range, with less than 5% variation in RMSE, confirming that the model is robust to the choice of weighting factors.

A key challenge in the SOC estimation is the limited availability of labeled data across diverse battery chemistries and aging conditions. The TL-PINN addressed this issue using TL that consisted of two phases. The first phase was a pre-training stage, in which a PINN was trained on a well-characterized source-domain battery dataset to learn the fundamental SOC estimation patterns across diverse operating conditions [39]. This enabled the model to acquire a robust representation of the underlying electrochemical dynamics governing SOC behavior. In the subsequent fine-tuning stage, the lower layers of the pretrained network were retained (frozen) to preserve these physics-informed features, while the higher layers were adapted to capture domain-specific variations. This layer-freezing strategy, consistent with prior findings that shallow layers encode transferable physical representations while deeper layers adapt to new domains [40], allows the model to achieve a balance between generalization and adaptability across target chemistries. The flowchart for training the TL-PINN is shown in Fig. 5 . The pre-trained model, M
s , can be expressed as follows:
(17)
x s
=
M s
u y
θ s
(18)
z s
=
f s
u y
θ s
where, θ
s denotes the pre-trained weight of the feature extraction network in the source domain, and z
s represents the extracted feature vector obtained from the input pair (u, y). Note that
f s
∙
is the data-driven feature extraction of the neural network. In the target domain, θ
t denotes the updated model parameters obtained during fine-tuning, while λ
t represents the physics-informed constraints applied to ensure consistency with governing equations.

The second phase was a fine-tuning phase in which the pretrained model was adapted to the target domain, such as new battery chemistries and aging conditions, using a limited dataset. Selective layer freezing was employed to retain the core knowledge learned during pre-training while allowing specific layers to be fine-tuned for better generalization to unseen battery types. The output voltage
y ̂
t
of the target domain can be predicted using the feature vector extracted as follows:
(19)
z t
=
f t
u
t − Δ k
k = 0
l − 1
y
t − Δ k
k = 0
l − 1
θ t
(20)
y ̂
t
=
g t
z t
θ t

here,
θ t
denotes the learnable network parameters in the target domain, initialized from
θ s
. During the fine tuning, the model was updated on the target dataset while simultaneously updating the physical parameters λ .

The loss function for TL can be expressed as follows:
(21)
L TL
=
ω TL
L s
+
1 −
ω TL
L t
where
L s
and
L t
denote the loss terms calculated from the source and target domains, respectively. Specifically,
L s
=
y s
−
y ̂
s
2 2
is the mean-squared error between the measured and predicted voltages in the source domain, while
L t
=
y t
−
y ̂
t
2 2
represents the same loss computed for the target domain samples. The weighting coefficient ω
TL controls the influence of source domain supervision to the overall training process.

To further minimize the distribution discrepancy between the two domains, we incorporated a domain adaptation term.
(22)
L DA
=
L t
+ βd
P s
P t
where d
P s
P t
measures the distance between the feature distributions of the source and target domains. In this study, d
∙
was implemented using the Maximum Mean Discrepancy metric, and β is a tunable coefficient that scales the adaptation strength. Consequently, the final objective function was optimized using stochastic gradient descent.

The experiments were conducted on an NVIDIA GeForce RTX 2060 GPU, an Intel Core i7-9700K CPU, and 64 GB RAM. A multi-stage training protocol was implemented, incorporating early stopping to prevent overfitting and improve generalization.

Table 2 summarizes the specifications of the training and validation datasets used in the TL-PINN experiments. Two TL scenarios were considered: NMC → NCA and NMC → LiPo. In both scenarios, the source domain training set comprised the Cycle and DST profiles. In the NCA target domain, the NN and HWFET profiles were used for training, whereas in the LiPo target domain, a mixture of profiles including the HWFET was employed. In both cases, the UDDS profile served as the validation dataset to assess the generalization. The notation “NMC → NCA” denotes fine-tuning a model pre-trained on the NMC source domain for the NCA target domain. It should be noted that only three target domain conditions were available for this study, which imposes a limitation on the scope of validation. Nonetheless, this experimental setup provides a reasonable demonstration of the adaptability of TL-PINN across chemistries and driving profiles.

The TL-PINN framework optimized the SOC estimation using adaptive learning rate decay, early stopping, and selective layer freezing to enhance the model efficiency and generalization. During training, the hyperparameters were tuned to balance the different loss components. The detailed formulation of the loss function is presented in Section 3.2.

The effectiveness of the proposed SOC estimator was evaluated using multiple quantitative metrics.
(23)
RMSE =
1 N
∑
i = 1
N
SOC
pred , i
−
SOC
true , i
2
(24)
MAPE
%
=
1 N
∑ i N
SOC
pred , i
−
SOC
true , i
SOC
true , i
× 100 %
where SO
C
pred , i
, and SO
C
true , i
denote the predicted and true SOC at time step i , respectively. N denotes the length of the dataset.

To enable a quantitative comparison with the proposed TL-PINN framework, two representative benchmark models were implemented: an EKF as a model-based estimator and an RNN as a data-driven baseline. These models were selected based on their widespread usage and represented two distinct paradigms in battery state estimation.

The EKF was constructed based on a first-order ECM. The OCV–SOC relationship was obtained from the experimental data via lookup table interpolation. The process noise covariance was set to Q = diag
1 ×
10
− 5
1 ×
10
− 6
, measurement noise covariance to R = 1 ×
10
− 4
, and initial state covariance to
P 0
= diag
0.01 0.01
.

The RNN model consisted of two hidden layers with 64 recurrent units each, using the rectified linear unit activation function. The input was a sequence of 30 time steps of past voltage and current measurements at a sampling rate of 10 Hz. The output was a single SOC value corresponding to the final time step. The model was trained using the Adam optimizer with a learning rate of 0.001 and a batch size of 32 for up to 200 epochs. Early stopping was applied based on validation loss, and a dropout (rate of 0.2 was used between layers to prevent overfitting.

In addition to EKF, RNN, and the proposed TL-PINN, we further included three mainstream deep learning models—LSTM, GRU, and CNN—as well as the Transformer architecture to broaden the benchmark. LSTM and GRU are recurrent models widely adopted for sequence learning in SOC estimation, CNNs provide efficient local feature extraction from time-series data, and Transformers capture long-range dependencies via attention mechanisms. These models represent representative and state-of-the-art data-driven approaches, enabling a fairer and more comprehensive comparison.

The estimation model was trained on the NMC dataset and subsequently evaluated on the LiPo and NCA datasets to examine the performance of the PINN for generalization across different battery chemistries. The results were compared against the conventional SOC estimation models, including the purely data-driven RNN model, model-based EKF model, and PINNs without TL. This comparison aimed to demonstrate the extent to which the TL-PINN adapted to unseen battery chemistries without extensive retraining.

Fig. 6 presents the baseline SOC estimation performance of conventional models, including EKF, RNN, LSTM, GRU, CNN, Transformer, and proposed PINN, when trained and tested within the same domain (NMC ➔ NMC). As shown in Fig. 6 (a), all models are able to track the ground truth SOC trajectory with high accuracy, demonstrating that domain-consistent training enables reliable prediction performance. The corresponding RMSE values in Fig. 6 (b) confirm this observation, with most models achieving errors below 1%. In particular, EKF and PINN exhibit the lowest errors, while purely data-driven approaches such as CNN and RNN show slightly larger deviations. These results highlight that traditional models are sufficiently effective under identical domain conditions; however, their robustness and generalization ability under cross-domain scenarios remain uncertain. This motivates the necessity of the proposed TL-PINN framework, which is specifically designed to maintain accuracy across varying battery chemistries and operating conditions. While all baseline models demonstrated strong performance when trained and evaluated within the same domain, their robustness is not guaranteed under domain shift. To further examine the generalization capability of different approaches, we evaluated the cross-domain SOC estimation performance from NMC to NCA, as presented in Fig. 7 . As shown in Fig. 7(a), conventional models such as RNN, LSTM, GRU, and Transformer exhibit significant deviations from the ground truth trajectory, highlighting their limited generalization ability under domain shift. The corresponding error distributions in Fig. 7(b) further confirm this tendency, with wide variance and large biases observed particularly for purely data-driven methods. EKF demonstrates relatively stable performance due to its model-based nature, but still suffers from noticeable bias under the mismatched battery chemistry. In contrast, the proposed PINN alleviates part of the error by embedding physical constraints, yet residual bias remains in specific segments. The TL-PINN achieves the most accurate and stable predictions, significantly reducing both variance and bias, thereby demonstrating superior adaptability across battery chemistries. These results validate the effectiveness of the proposed TL-PINN framework for cross-domain SOC estimation.

To further assess cross-domain robustness, we evaluated the more challenging transfer scenario from NMC to LiPo, which exhibits more distinct electrochemical characteristics than NCA. Fig. 8 presents the corresponding SOC estimation results, allowing us to examine whether the proposed TL-PINN can consistently adapt to diverse battery chemistries. Similar to the NMC → NCA case, conventional data-driven models and EKF exhibited significant deviations when applied to the unseen LiPo chemistry. As shown in Fig. 8(a), RNN, LSTM, and GRU suffered from large drifts and fluctuations, while EKF and CNN displayed persistent biases. Although the PINN provided more stable predictions than purely data-driven baselines, its accuracy was still constrained by chemistry-dependent variations not fully captured by the governing equations. In contrast, the proposed TL-PINN consistently outperformed all baselines, with the error distribution in Fig. 8(b) tightly concentrated near zero. Comparing the two transfer scenarios (NMC → NCA and NMC → LiPo), TL-PINN demonstrates strong adaptability across distinct chemistries, underscoring its robustness for realistic cross-domain SOC estimation tasks.

Overall, the results highlighted the effectiveness of the TL-PINN in improving cross-battery generalization. By integrating physics-informed constraints with TL, the TL-PINN significantly reduced the prediction error while maintaining stability across different battery chemistries.

Table 3 presents a comparison between the true battery parameters and the estimated battery parameters obtained using the PINN model for different battery chemistries, including NMC, NCA, and LiPo. In this study, the true battery parameters refer to reference parameters identified from experimental charge and discharge profile of each datasets. These parameters were obtained by fitting the first-order ECM to the measure voltage and current signal [41]. The accuracy of the PINN model was assessed by computing the percentage error between the estimated and true values.

The results indicated that the PINN model maintained an error margin below 5% across all parameters and battery chemistries, demonstrating high accuracy in parameter estimation. Specifically, for the NMC chemistry, the maximum observed error was 3.99% for R
p , whereas for NCA, the highest error was 4.5% for R p. Similarly, for the LiPo chemistry, the highest error recorded was 4.12% for C
p . These results confirmed that the PINN model provided consistent and reliable estimations without significant deviations from the true values.

The low estimation error across the different battery types suggested that the PINN framework effectively captured the underlying electrochemical dynamics and generalized well across multiple chemistries. The results further validated the robustness of the proposed model, indicating its potential applicability to real-world battery state estimation tasks, where precise parameter identification is crucial for accurate SOC estimation and optimal battery management.

In practical applications, acquiring large amounts of labeled SOC data can be challenging because of the cost and complexity of extensive battery testing. Therefore, in Section 4.3, we evaluate how the prediction performance changes as the amount of target-domain data used in the transfer-learning stage increases. Here the training data amount refers only to the fraction of target-domain employed during TL-PINN fine-turning, while all baseline models remain trained exclusively on the source-domain dataset to ensure a fair comparison and to isolate the benefit of transfer learning. The subsets used in this analysis correspond to the target-domain training and validation dataset described in Table 2. Specifically, Subset 1 contains the first 10% of the target domain data, Subset 2 contains the first 30%, and Subset 3 comprises the entire 50% dataset. These samples were selected in chronological order to reflect realistic battery cycling sequences. Figs. 9 and 10
, indicated that as the amount of training data in the target domain increased, the performance gradually improved toward the baseline. Fig. 9 corresponds to the NCA target domain, whereas Fig. 10 presents the results for the LiPo target domain. The three panels represent the prediction accuracy when using (a) one subset, (b) two subsets, and (c) the full training dataset.

When only a small portion of target-domain data was available, as shown in Fig. 9(a), the purely data-driven models showed considerable instability. The RNN exhibited the largest fluctuations and bias across the time horizon, while the LSTM and GRU also displayed wide error dispersion despite their recurrent structures. The Transformer captured long-range dependencies but suffered from inconsistent convergence under scarce data. CNNs provided relatively smoother estimates by extracting local features, yet their errors remained larger than physics-informed approaches. The PINN improved stability through physical constraints, but its error variance was still noticeable. In contrast, the TL-PINN fine-tuned using only Subset 1 achieved the lowest bias and the narrowest error distribution, demonstrating robust generalization and accurate tracking even in highly data-limited regimes.

When the target-domain data used for fine-tuning was increased to two subsets, as shown in Fig. 9(b), the estimation errors decreased across all models. LSTM, GRU, and CNN benefitted from the additional data, although they continued to exhibit broader error ranges compared with the PINN-based models. The Transformer showed moderate improvement but retained sporadic deviations. TL-PINN consistently outperformed all other methods, exhibiting both minimal bias and the smallest interquartile range, while the PINN also improved but remained less stable than the transfer-learning variant.

Finally, when the full target-domain dataset was used in Fig. 9(c), all models benefitted from the increased data volume, resulting in overall performance gains. Nevertheless, a clear performance hierarchy persisted: TL-PINN provided the most accurate and consistent estimates across chemistries and operating conditions, followed by the PINN. CNN and Transformer offered competitive performance but with slightly larger dispersion, whereas the recurrent models (RNN, LSTM, GRU) continued to show relatively high variability.

These results confirm that combining physical priors with cross-domain knowledge significantly enhances sample efficiency and provides a strong inductive bias that stabilizes learning under data-constrained or unseen operational conditions.

Fig. 10 presents the SOC estimation results on the LiPo target domain for different training dataset sizes. Consistent with the NCA results in Fig. 9, all models benefited from increasing the number of training subsets, but their relative performance differences remained evident.

In the low-data regime with only one subset in Fig. 10(a), the purely recurrent models (RNN, LSTM, GRU) suffered from large variance and bias, with the RNN showing the most unstable trajectories and LSTM/GRU only partially alleviating these fluctuations. The Transformer captured longer temporal dependencies but produced inconsistent convergence and higher dispersion under scarce data. CNNs achieved smoother estimates than the recurrent models but still lagged behind physics-informed approaches. By contrast, the PINN reduced bias through embedded physical constraints, and the TL-PINN achieved the most accurate predictions with narrow error bounds, demonstrating strong generalization from the source domain despite limited target data.

With two subsets available in Fig. 10(b), all data-driven models improved moderately, but their error distributions remained wider than those of the PINN-based methods. The Transformer and CNN became more competitive yet still showed occasional deviations. TL-PINN maintained clear superiority, exhibiting the lowest mean error and the tightest error distribution, while the PINN followed with moderate improvements.

When the full training dataset was used in Fig. 10(c), all models converged toward higher accuracy; however, the performance hierarchy persisted. TL-PINN delivered the best overall results with the lowest error and narrowest interquartile range, followed by PINN. CNN and Transformer produced competitive estimates but with slightly larger dispersion, whereas RNN, LSTM, and GRU continued to exhibit higher variability. These results confirm that the TL-PINN framework effectively transfers knowledge to previously unseen chemistries such as LiPo, maintaining robustness even under limited data conditions.

Fig. 11 presents the RMSE (%) values for different SOC estimation models for varying training dataset sizes. The TL-PINN consistently achieved the lowest RMSE, confirming its superior TL capability compared with other methods. In contrast, the RNN exhibited the highest RMSE, particularly with only one and two subsets, indicating poor generalization under limited data. The PINN, augmented with physical constraints, performed better than the RNN but remained significantly behind the TL-PINN in low-data scenarios. As the training set size increased, all models showed improved performance; however, the TL-PINN retained a clear advantage. When the full dataset was used, the TL-PINN still outperformed the PINN and RNN in terms of mean error and error variance. Comparing the battery chemistries, LiPo (blue bars) consistently produced a higher RMSE than NCA (green bars), suggesting greater transfer difficulty. Across all dataset sizes, the RNN struggled the most with LiPo, underscoring the limitations of pure data-driven models under chemistry shifts.

Fig. 12 shows the loss convergences of the TL-PINN (blue), PINN (red), and RNN (green) over 200 training epochs. The TL-PINN converged fastest—achieving significantly lower loss within the first 50 epochs and stabilizing quickly. The PINN also converged steadily, albeit slightly slower, ultimately reaching a similar final loss and demonstrating reliable training performance. In contrast, the RNN converged more slowly and exhibited higher fluctuations, still showing noticeable variation after 100 epochs, indicating slower optimization and lower efficiency. The inference time comparison revealed that the RNN required the longest execution time of approximately 5.2 ms per inference owing to its complex structure. The PINN and TL-PINN were considerably faster, with inference times of approximately 2.3 ms and 2.4 ms, respectively. This highlighted the way physics-informed constraints enabled an efficient and stable model performance with minimal computational overhead.

To further clarify the contribution of transfer learning beyond the PINN structure, we conducted an ablation study comparing pretrained initialization against random initialization. As summarized in Table 4 , pretrained initialization converged significantly faster and achieved lower RMSE and MAE, whereas random initialization required substantially more epochs and exhibited unstable training behavior. These findings demonstrate that the transfer learning mechanism meaningfully enhances the robustness and efficiency of the TL-PINN framework.

Battery voltage and current signals are often affected by sensor noise that can degrade the SOC estimation accuracy. To evaluate the robustness of the TL-PINN under these conditions, we injected Gaussian noise into the voltage and current measurements and analyzed its impact on estimation performance. The noise levels were set to 5% for the current and 1% for the voltage, to reflect realistic sensor inaccuracies.

The SOC estimation results under 5% current measurement noise for the NCA and LiPo chemistries are presented in Fig. 13 . The rolling RMSE plots (left) and error distributions (right) show the noise sensitivity of each model. The EKF maintained rolling RMSE values below approximately 1.8 but still exhibited characteristic bias due to its fixed process and measurement noise assumptions. The RNN showed the largest instability with high error variance across the operating range, while the remaining data-driven models exhibited varying levels of fluctuation depending on the noise sensitivity of their architectures.

Among recurrent models, the LSTM and GRU reduced fluctuations compared with the vanilla RNN, but both still displayed noticeable dispersion in their error distributions, confirming limited robustness to measurement noise. The Transformer achieved more consistent predictions than recurrent models, yet its error spread remained broader than physics-informed approaches. The CNN achieved stable performance in some regions but showed bias accumulation over time, indicating insufficient adaptation to noise-corrupted signals.

By contrast, the PINN significantly outperformed purely data-driven models by embedding physical consistency, yielding lower rolling RMSE and compact error distributions. Nevertheless, the proposed TL-PINN achieved the most robust performance across both chemistries, maintaining the lowest bias and variance under noisy conditions. These results confirm that combining physics-informed constraints with transfer learning enhances resilience against sensor noise, ensuring stable SOC estimation in practical applications. To further quantify these results, Table 5 presents the averaged RMSE values under both noise-free and noisy conditions.

Table 5 summarizes the SOC estimation performance under noise-free and 5% measurement noise conditions across all benchmark models (EKF, RNN, LSTM, GRU, Transformer, CNN, and PINN) for the NMC, NCA, and LiPo chemistries. Under noise-free conditions, the EKF achieved the lowest RMSE (∼0.11–0.12) owing to its reliance on predefined system dynamics, while the PINN also performed competitively with values of 0.12–0.16. Among the purely data-driven models, recurrent approaches (RNN, LSTM, GRU) and the Transformer achieved reasonable accuracy, but their RMSE values of 0.18–0.22 were consistently higher than those of physics-informed methods. When 5% noise was introduced, the EKF performance degraded sharply, with RMSE increasing to 0.75–3.31, indicating high sensitivity to measurement errors. Data-driven models such as RNN, LSTM, GRU, Transformer, and CNN showed moderate robustness but still suffered error growth. By contrast, the PINN consistently maintained the lowest RMSE under noise of 0.39–1.20, confirming that embedding physics-based constraints enhances noise resilience and stabilizes SOC estimation across chemistries.

Finally, a standard PINN was adopted in this study to provide a clear and interpretable foundation for evaluating the benefits of transfer learning. This choice ensured that the observed improvements could be attributed directly to the TL mechanism rather than additional architectural complexities. Although advanced PINN variants (e.g., adaptive weighting, domain decomposition, operator learning) may further improve computational efficiency, the standard PINN already provided sufficient accuracy for the present scope, and their integration with TL is left as future work.

In this study, we proposed a TL-PINN framework for SOC estimation in lithium-ion batteries, targeting scenarios with limited data availability and uncertain battery conditions. The framework combined the strengths of the PINN and TL to address the challenges of generalization, limited datasets, and robustness. The proposed TL-PINN integrated three steps: (1) a PINN-based SOC estimation model that incorporated battery electrochemical equations for physically consistent predictions, (2) a TL mechanism that adapted a pretrained model to new battery chemistries and operating conditions, and (3) a hybrid loss function that balanced data-driven learning, physical consistency, and domain adaptation. The experimental results demonstrated that the proposed framework outperformed conventional SOC estimation methods, achieving a lower RMSE, improved cross-chemistry adaptability, and increased robustness to sensor noise. Under data-scarce conditions, the data-driven RNN model failed to adapt to the target domain, yielding a high RMSE of 10.9%. In contrast, the proposed TL-PINN maintained a low RMSE of 4.02% while achieving a fast inference time of 2.4 ms. Moreover, under sensing noise conditions, the PINN consistently maintained an error rate below 2% across all datasets. This represented an average improvement of 65.69% and 50.52% over the EKF and RNN, respectively. These results validated the efficacy of the TL-PINN in improving the SOC estimation under practical constraints.

Seonri Hong: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Software, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Hyejin Kim: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Investigation. Jonghoon Kim: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Investigation. Jongbok Baek: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the framework of the research and development program of the Korea Institute of Energy Research (C6- 2410).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel method for estimating the state of health of lithium-ion batteries based on physics-informed neural network
